title: Bluetooth, NFC, and IoT Wireless
description: Test your knowledge of Bluetooth Classic, BLE, pairing processes, Bluetooth security threats, NFC, RFID, and IoT wireless protocols like Zigbee and Z-Wave.
starterCode:
# BLUETOOTH, NFC, AND IoT WIRELESS EXERCISE

## Part 1: Bluetooth Classic

1. Bluetooth operates in the _______ GHz ISM frequency band.

2. Bluetooth Classic uses a technique called frequency _______ spread spectrum (FHSS).

3. Bluetooth Classic hops between _______ different channels.

4. Bluetooth Classic is designed for _______ data streaming like audio.

5. The maximum range of Bluetooth Class 2 devices is approximately _______ meters.

## Part 2: Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)

6. BLE was introduced in Bluetooth version _______.

7. BLE is optimized for _______ power consumption and periodic data transfers.

8. BLE uses _______ advertising channels to announce device presence.

9. BLE is commonly used in _______ devices, fitness trackers, and beacons.

10. BLE supports a mesh networking topology since Bluetooth version _______.

## Part 3: Bluetooth Pairing and Security

11. The Bluetooth pairing process establishes a shared _______ key between devices.

12. Secure Simple Pairing (SSP) was introduced in Bluetooth _______.

13. SSP supports four association models: Numeric Comparison, Just Works, Passkey Entry, and _______.

14. _______ is an attack where unsolicited messages are sent to a Bluetooth device.

15. _______ is a more serious attack that steals data from a Bluetooth device without consent.

16. _______ involves eavesdropping on Bluetooth communications by exploiting key negotiation.

17. Devices should disable Bluetooth _______ mode when not actively pairing.

## Part 4: NFC and RFID

18. NFC stands for Near-Field _______.

19. NFC operates at a frequency of _______ MHz.

20. NFC has a maximum communication range of approximately _______ centimeters.

21. NFC supports three modes: reader/writer, peer-to-peer, and card _______.

22. RFID stands for Radio-Frequency _______.

23. Passive RFID tags do not require their own _______ source.

24. Active RFID tags have their own battery and offer _______ range than passive tags.

## Part 5: Zigbee and Z-Wave

25. Zigbee is based on the IEEE _______ standard.

26. Zigbee operates on 2.4 GHz and supports _______ network topology.

27. Z-Wave operates in the sub-_______ MHz frequency band (around 908 MHz in the US).

28. Z-Wave is primarily designed for home _______ applications.

29. Zigbee supports up to _______ of nodes in a single network (theoretical maximum).

30. Both Zigbee and Z-Wave are designed for _______ data rate, low power IoT applications.
solution:
# SOLUTION: BLUETOOTH, NFC, AND IoT WIRELESS

## Part 1: Bluetooth Classic

1. Bluetooth operates in the **2.4** GHz ISM frequency band. ✓

2. Bluetooth Classic uses a technique called frequency **hopping** spread spectrum (FHSS). ✓

3. Bluetooth Classic hops between **79** different channels. ✓

4. Bluetooth Classic is designed for **continuous** data streaming like audio. ✓

5. The maximum range of Bluetooth Class 2 devices is approximately **10** meters. ✓

## Part 2: Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)

6. BLE was introduced in Bluetooth version **4.0**. ✓

7. BLE is optimized for **low** power consumption and periodic data transfers. ✓

8. BLE uses **3** advertising channels to announce device presence. ✓

9. BLE is commonly used in **IoT** devices, fitness trackers, and beacons. ✓

10. BLE supports a mesh networking topology since Bluetooth version **5.0**. ✓

## Part 3: Bluetooth Pairing and Security

11. The Bluetooth pairing process establishes a shared **link** key between devices. ✓

12. Secure Simple Pairing (SSP) was introduced in Bluetooth **2.1**. ✓

13. SSP supports four association models: Numeric Comparison, Just Works, Passkey Entry, and **Out of Band**. ✓

14. **Bluejacking** is an attack where unsolicited messages are sent to a Bluetooth device. ✓

15. **Bluesnarfing** is a more serious attack that steals data from a Bluetooth device without consent. ✓

16. **Bluebugging** involves eavesdropping on Bluetooth communications by exploiting key negotiation. ✓

17. Devices should disable Bluetooth **discoverable** mode when not actively pairing. ✓

## Part 4: NFC and RFID

18. NFC stands for Near-Field **Communication**. ✓

19. NFC operates at a frequency of **13.56** MHz. ✓

20. NFC has a maximum communication range of approximately **10** centimeters. ✓

21. NFC supports three modes: reader/writer, peer-to-peer, and card **emulation**. ✓

22. RFID stands for Radio-Frequency **Identification**. ✓

23. Passive RFID tags do not require their own **power** source. ✓

24. Active RFID tags have their own battery and offer **greater** range than passive tags. ✓

## Part 5: Zigbee and Z-Wave

25. Zigbee is based on the IEEE **802.15.4** standard. ✓

26. Zigbee operates on 2.4 GHz and supports **mesh** network topology. ✓

27. Z-Wave operates in the sub-**1 GHz** MHz frequency band (around 908 MHz in the US). ✓

28. Z-Wave is primarily designed for home **automation** applications. ✓

29. Zigbee supports up to **65,000** of nodes in a single network (theoretical maximum). ✓

30. Both Zigbee and Z-Wave are designed for **low** data rate, low power IoT applications. ✓
hints: Bluetooth operates in the same frequency band as Wi-Fi — the 2.4 GHz ISM band that is shared with many wireless technologies.
Bluetooth Classic hops between 79 channels at 1600 hops per second — this is frequency hopping spread spectrum (FHSS).
BLE was a major feature of Bluetooth 4.0 — it was designed specifically for IoT and wearable devices with low power needs.
Bluetooth attacks have colorful names: Bluejacking sends unsolicited messages (annoying but harmless), Bluesnarfing steals data (serious threat).
Always disable discoverable mode on Bluetooth when not pairing — this prevents attackers from finding your device.
NFC operates at 13.56 MHz and works within about 10 centimeters — this very short range is actually a security feature.
NFC's card emulation mode lets your phone act as a contactless payment card — think of mobile payment systems.
RFID stands for Radio-Frequency Identification — passive tags get power from the reader's signal, no battery needed.
Zigbee is based on IEEE 802.15.4 and supports mesh topologies — it can theoretically support up to 65,000 nodes.
Z-Wave uses sub-1 GHz frequencies (around 908 MHz in the US) specifically to avoid interference with 2.4 GHz devices like Wi-Fi.

testCases.description: Should identify mesh topology for Zigbee
Should identify Z-Wave home automation purpose
